GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "How these Billy Blue graduates took their careers global", "Where creative talent meets training, anything is possible.", "There are few degrees that can send your CV off in quite as diverse a trajectory as the field of design. And few schools are as perfectly poised to turn that training into a business as Billy Blue College of Design at Torrens University Australia. Through online and on-campus study, Billy Blue is equipping their graduates not only with technical nous and international connections but the knowledge and skills to become entrepreneurial mavericks (not just artists) on a truly global scale. With a roster of alumni that rivals any design school around the world, success at Billy Blue can take you anywhere. Victorian Communication Design graduate Jake Ross is an illustrator, creative director and mural artist (his latest piece was a large-scale painting with musician Angus Stone) and works in a variety of mediums. His signature playful, but thoughtful style (see more of it @jakeross.art), mixing the light with the dark, has seen him collaborate with brands like VB, Bonds and Playboy, as well as Justin Beiber. "Billy Blue instilled a confidence within me early on. They exposed me to the word 'collaboration.' They really grow each individual as a creative, and empower those creatives to really change the world. They ensure each individual has a real global focus – I can't count how many international collaborations I encountered there," he told Vogue. Ross' advice for future students, and all creative types in general is simple but valuable. "Always carry a notepad and pencil. Sleeping, in the car on the way back from a surf, over breakfast... I've had many valuable thoughts come while I'm not working," he explains. Graphic designer Nyasha Chakaingesu received a scholarship to study Digital Media Design at Billy Blue on the Melbourne campus. She went on to intern at the United Nations. "Billy Blue really pushed my thinking process. I'm willing to take more risks, be disruptive, flip the script and think how to better design by knowing the people you are designing for – empathising with them and designing for the greater good of our people, community and nation," Chakaingesu explained to Vogue. Her advice for design students is two-fold. Firstly, if you don't know what you want to do, start with a 12 month diploma. "It allows you to sample different areas of design. That's what I did and it's how I found I really enjoyed 3D design," she explained. Secondly, don't be afraid to share. "Fear can hold you back – especially for creatives. Post your sketches or images or research. You have a unique voice and it matters," she adds. Chakaingesu regularly post sketches, paintings and designs on @nyashacreative. Hong Kong-born illustrator Stellar Leuna can count Prada, Tiffany and Co, Netflix and GQ Germany in her client list, since graduating Communication Design from at Billy Blue in 2012. "Learning about design really shaped how I perceive the world. The lecturers that taught me had a really good grasp on what makes an image successful because they were all still working in the industry," Leuna told Vogue. She says a focus on employability at Billy Blue helped teach her to use the skills as a profession. "I was taught how to communicate with clients and approach things professionally - that was one of the most valuable things I learnt there that can be adapted to being an illustrator as opposed to a straight-forward graphic designer," she added. Right now, Leuna is working on a new body of work to hopefully exhibit when the Covid-19 lockdown ends. You can follow her progress on Instagram at @stellarleuna. If you want to join this impressive and dynamic field of Billy Blue alum, working amongst the full breadth of the diverse design community, and jump start your own adventure into the business of design, with all of its endless possibilities and applications, Billy Blue are hosting a Virtual Open Day starting 19 May."
